nativeRange.habitat: Coral and silty reef areas, lagoons, and seaward reefs at depths of approximately 4–60 meters; tends to favor areas with mixed coral and rubble rather than dense coral growth

diet.notes: Has been documented eating algae, sponges, and small invertebrates in the wild. In aquariums, generally advised to offer a varied diet including spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations containing sponge matter, mysis shrimp, and other meaty foods. Feeding 2–3 times per day tends to support good health and coloration.

summary: A large, striking angelfish from the Red Sea and western Indian Ocean, generally considered suitable for experienced reef hobbyists with very large systems. Juveniles display vivid blue and white barring; adults develop a bold yellow vertical bar with a transparent yellowish tail.
careNotes:
Pomacanthus maculosus is among the larger angelfish kept in home aquariums, commonly reaching 40–50 cm in captivity; FishBase records a maximum of 50 cm SL with a reported lifespan of up to 36 years in the wild. A single specimen per tank is generally advised due to territorial behavior toward conspecifics and similarly shaped fish.

Temperature: Sources disagree on the preferred range. LiveAquaria states 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase wild data gives 24.8–29.3°C; Reeflings recommends 24–28°C. The averaged range used here is approximately 24–28°C; the lower end of LiveAquaria's range (22°C) may reflect older guidance and is below the wild-data minimum. Review recommended.

Reef safety: Sources are split. LiveAquaria rates it 'with caution' (prone to nipping stony and soft corals and clam mantles); Reeflings rates it outright 'no'. In practice, individuals vary — some keepers report successful reef cohabitation with less palatable corals, but the species tends to pick at sessile invertebrates. Caution is warranted.

Care level: LiveAquaria rates it 'easy'; Reeflings rates it 'moderate to difficult'. The intermediate rating here reflects the species' large adult size, substantial tank requirements (~700+ liters), and tendency to nip invertebrates. The fish itself is generally hardy and accepts prepared foods readily, which contributes to a lower individual difficulty, but total system demands are higher.

Minimum tank size: Varies by source — LiveAquaria recommends 250 gal (946 L), Reeflings recommends 500 L (132 gal), and general hobby consensus suggests 180–250 gal. The averaged figure of ~710 L is used; a larger system is generally preferred for long-term wellbeing given the fish's adult size.

Breeding: Members of Pomacanthidae are hermaphroditic and rarely bred in captivity. No documented captive breeding success for this species has been widely reported.
breedingNotes: Pomacanthids are sequential hermaphrodites and are generally considered extremely difficult to breed in captivity. Captive breeding of P. maculosus has not been reliably documented; wild-caught specimens are the norm in the trade.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm SL), temperature range (24.8–29.3°C), native range, taxonomy, and lifespan (up to 36 years reported). Temperature minimum notably higher than LiveAquaria (24.8°C vs 22.2°C).

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Third cross-check source. Confirmed size 50 cm, temperature 24–28°C, salinity SG 1.023–1.026. Rated care level 'moderate to difficult' (vs LiveAquaria 'easy') and reef safe 'no' (vs LiveAquaria 'with caution'). Tank minimum given as 500 L, lower than LiveAquaria's 250 gal (946 L). Used in averaging temperature, salinity, tank volume, and care level.

fish.breedingNotes: Pomacanthids are sequential hermaphrodites; captive breeding of P. maculosus has not been reliably documented. Wild-caught specimens are standard in the aquarium trade.
